news_summary: Advancements in quantum computing and human expertise outperforming AI on complex tasks highlight significant progress in technology and problem-solving capabilities.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière d'informatique quantique et le fait que l'expertise humaine surpasse l'IA dans des tâches complexes mettent en évidence des progrès significatifs dans le domaine technologique et en matière de capacités de résolution de problèmes.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la computación cuántica y el hecho de que la experiencia humana supere a la IA en tareas complejas ponen de manifiesto un progreso significativo en la tecnología y en la capacidad para resolver problemas.

articles.title: “Giant superatoms” could finally solve quantum computing’s biggest problem
articles.summary: In the pursuit of powerful and stable quantum computers, researchers at Chalmers University of Technology, Sweden, have developed the theory for an entirely new quantum system – based on the novel concept of ‘giant superatoms’. This breakthrough enables quantum information to be protected, controlled, and distributed in new ways and could be a key step towards building quantum computers at scale.
articles.content:
Researchers at Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden have introduced a new theoretical design for quantum systems based on what they call "giant superatoms." This concept offers a fresh way to protect, control, and share quantum information, potentially bringing scientists closer to building large-scale quantum computers.

Quantum computers are expected to transform fields like drug discovery and encryption by solving problems that are far beyond the reach of conventional machines. However, progress has been limited by a major challenge known as decoherence. This occurs when quantum bits, or qubits, lose their information due to interactions with their surroundings. Even small amounts of electromagnetic noise can disrupt the fragile quantum states needed for computation.

"Quantum systems are extraordinarily powerful but also extremely fragile. The key to making them useful is learning how to control their interaction with the surrounding environment," says Lei Du, postdoctoral researcher in applied quantum technology at Chalmers.

Lei Du is the lead author of a study that outlines this new type of quantum system. The design is built around giant superatoms, which combine several important features. These systems reduce decoherence, remain stable, and consist of multiple interconnected "atoms" that function together as a single unit.

What Are Giant Superatoms

Giant superatoms bring together two previously separate ideas in quantum physics: giant atoms and superatoms. While each has been studied on its own, this is the first time they have been merged into a single system. These structures behave like atoms but are not found in nature. Instead, they are engineered by scientists (see fact box below).

Giant Atoms and Their "Quantum Echo"

The idea of giant atoms was first introduced by researchers at Chalmers over a decade ago and is now widely used in the field. A giant atom is typically designed as a qubit (which is the smallest unit of quantum information). Unlike ordinary atoms, it connects to light or sound waves at multiple, physically separated points. This allows it to interact with its environment in several places at once, helping it preserve quantum information.

"Waves that leave one connection point can travel through the environment and return to affect the atom at another point -- similar to hearing an echo of your own voice before you've finished speaking. This self-interaction leads to highly beneficial quantum effects, reduces decoherence and gives the system a form of memory of past interactions," explains Anton Frisk Kockum, Associate Professor of Applied Quantum Physics at Chalmers and co-author of the study.

Extending Entanglement Across Distances

Although giant atoms have improved understanding of quantum behavior, they have had limitations when it comes to entanglement. Entanglement allows multiple qubits to share a single quantum state and act as one coordinated system, which is essential for powerful quantum computers.

To overcome this limitation, the research team combined giant atoms with the concept of superatoms. A superatom consists of several natural atoms that share the same quantum state and behave collectively as one larger atom.

This combination is expected to make it easier to create complex quantum states needed for quantum communication, networks, and highly sensitive measurement systems.

"A giant superatom may be envisaged as multiple giant atoms working together as a single entity, exhibiting a non-local interaction between light and matter. This enables quantum information from multiple qubits to be stored and controlled within one unit, without the need for increasingly complex surrounding circuitry," explains Lei Du.

"Giant superatoms open the door to entirely new capabilities, giving us a powerful new toolbox. They allow us to control quantum information and create entanglement in ways that were previously extremely difficult, or even impossible," says Janine Splettstoesser, Professor of Applied Quantum Physics at Chalmers and co-author of the study.

Toward Scalable and Practical Quantum Systems

This work creates new possibilities for building quantum systems that are both scalable and reliable. The researchers plan to move from theory toward actually constructing these systems. Their design could also be integrated with other quantum technologies, serving as a building block for connecting different types of quantum platforms.

"There is currently strong interest in hybrid approaches, in which different quantum systems work together, because each has its own strengths," says Anton Frisk Kockum. "Our research shows that smart design can reduce the need for increasingly complex hardware and giant superatoms are bringing us one step closer to practically applicable quantum technology."

Controlling Quantum Information Flow

More on: Methods for protecting, controlling and distributing quantum information

The study shows that the way giant superatoms interact with light depends on their internal quantum states. This discovery gives researchers greater control over how quantum information moves through a system. They describe two different ways of connecting these structures to achieve useful outcomes.

In one setup, several giant superatoms are closely linked in a specific arrangement. This allows them to pass quantum states between each other without decoherence, meaning no information is lost.

In another setup, the atoms are spaced farther apart but connected in a carefully tuned way so that waves remain synchronized. This makes it possible to direct quantum signals and distribute entanglement over long distances.

Understanding Giant Atoms and Superatoms

Superatoms and giant atoms are engineered systems that behave like atoms rather than naturally occurring ones.

A superatom is a quantum system made up of multiple natural atoms that share a single quantum state and respond to light as one entity.

A giant atom, on the other hand, connects to light or sound waves at several separate points in space. It is called "giant" because it is larger than the wavelength of the light it interacts with.

Giant atoms have defined energy levels and follow the rules of quantum mechanics, yet they can reach sizes of up to millimeters, making them visible to the naked eye. Through electromagnetic or acoustic waves, they can interact with their surroundings at multiple locations at the same time. One way to picture this is as a single atom linked to a wave at several distant points. This unusual setup allows the atom to be influenced by the waves it produces.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant theoretical breakthrough in quantum computing by researchers at Chalmers University, introducing 'giant superatoms' that could solve the major problem of decoherence, enabling scalable, stable quantum computers. This advancement has broad implications for fields like drug discovery and encryption, representing a meaningful step forward in technology with substantial real-world impact and detailed explanation.
articles.personality_title: New 'Giant Superatoms' design could make quantum computers more stable
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers can solve problems regular computers cannot, but they have a big problem: their quantum bits, called qubits, easily lose information because they are very sensitive to noise and disturbances. This problem is known as decoherence and has slowed down progress in building large, useful quantum computers.

**What happened** – Researchers at Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden have created a new theoretical design called "giant superatoms." These are engineered groups of atoms that act like one big atom and can protect and control quantum information better than before. The design combines two ideas, giant atoms and superatoms, to keep qubits stable and connected without losing information.

**Impact** – This new approach could help overcome the main obstacle of decoherence by reducing information loss and allowing many qubits to work together as one system. It makes controlling and sharing quantum information easier, which is important for building bigger and more reliable quantum computers. This could speed up advances in areas like medicine and secure communication.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to move from theory to building real giant superatom systems. They also want to combine this design with other quantum technologies to create hybrid systems that use the strengths of different quantum parts. This could lead to practical quantum computers that work outside of labs.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have designed "giant superatoms" that could protect quantum information better and help build stable, large-scale quantum computers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les « superatomes géants » pourraient stabiliser les ordinateurs quantiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent résoudre des problèmes que les ordinateurs classiques ne peuvent pas, mais ils ont un gros problème : leurs bits quantiques, appelés qubits, perdent facilement leurs informations à cause du bruit et des perturbations. Ce problème, appelé décohérence, ralentit la construction d’ordinateurs quantiques utiles et grands.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de technologie de Chalmers en Suède ont créé un nouveau modèle théorique nommé « superatomes géants ». Ce sont des groupes d’atomes conçus pour agir comme un seul gros atome, capable de mieux protéger et contrôler l’information quantique. Cette idée combine deux concepts, les atomes géants et les superatomes, pour garder les qubits stables et connectés sans perdre d’informations.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode pourrait aider à résoudre le principal problème de décohérence en réduisant la perte d’information et en permettant à plusieurs qubits de fonctionner ensemble comme un seul système. Cela facilite le contrôle et le partage de l’information quantique, ce qui est important pour construire des ordinateurs quantiques plus grands et plus fiables. Cela pourrait accélérer les progrès dans des domaines comme la médecine et la communication sécurisée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de passer de la théorie à la construction réelle de systèmes de superatomes géants. Ils veulent aussi combiner ce modèle avec d’autres technologies quantiques pour créer des systèmes hybrides qui utilisent les forces de différentes parties quantiques. Cela pourrait mener à des ordinateurs quantiques pratiques fonctionnant hors des laboratoires.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont conçu des « superatomes géants » capables de mieux protéger l’information quantique et d’aider à construire des ordinateurs quantiques stables et à grande échelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Los "superátomos gigantes" podrían hacer que las computadoras cuánticas sean más estables
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas pueden resolver problemas que las computadoras normales no pueden, pero tienen un gran problema: sus bits cuánticos, llamados qubits, pierden información fácilmente debido al ruido y las perturbaciones. Este problema se llama decoherencia y ha frenado el desarrollo de computadoras cuánticas grandes y útiles.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tecnológica de Chalmers en Suecia han creado un nuevo diseño teórico llamado "superátomos gigantes". Estos son grupos de átomos diseñados para actuar como un solo átomo grande que puede proteger y controlar mejor la información cuántica. La idea combina dos conceptos, átomos gigantes y superátomos, para mantener los qubits estables y conectados sin perder información.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo enfoque podría ayudar a superar el problema principal de la decoherencia al reducir la pérdida de información y permitir que muchos qubits trabajen juntos como un solo sistema. Facilita el control y la distribución de la información cuántica, algo clave para construir computadoras cuánticas más grandes y confiables. Esto podría acelerar avances en áreas como la medicina y la comunicación segura.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean pasar de la teoría a construir sistemas reales de superátomos gigantes. También quieren combinar este diseño con otras tecnologías cuánticas para crear sistemas híbridos que usen las fortalezas de diferentes partes cuánticas. Esto podría llevar a computadoras cuánticas prácticas que funcionen fuera de los laboratorios.

**Frase clave** – Científicos diseñaron "superátomos gigantes" que podrían proteger mejor la información cuántica y ayudar a construir computadoras cuánticas estables y a gran escala.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of several interconnected glowing orbs—each orb composed of multiple smaller luminous spheres clustered tightly together—floating above a calm, softly rippling water surface; delicate waves of light and sound gently link the orbs at multiple points, creating an intricate network of shimmering, echoing connections that symbolize stable, collective quantum systems working in harmony.

articles.title: Human scientists trounce the best AI agents on complex tasks
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01199-zState-of-the-industry report finds that, despite the limitations of artificial-intelligence systems, researchers have embraced them.
articles.content:
The number of publications in life, physical and Earth sciences that mention AI grew by a factor of almost 30 from 2010 to 2025.Credit: 4kodiak/Getty

In an indication of how quickly scientists are embracing artificial intelligence, the number of publications in the natural sciences that mention AI grew by almost 30-fold from 2010 to 2025, according to an influential annual state-of-the-field report.

The proportion of publications in any given natural-sciences field that mention AI ranges from 6% to 9% (see ‘AI paper boom’), according to the Artificial Intelligence Index Report 2026, released today by the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI at Stanford University in California1.

“Scientists have really embraced this AI era,” says computer scientist Yolanda Gil at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles, who led this year’s index report (see ‘Fun AI facts’).

Source: Artificial Intelligence Index Report 2026

Alongside the boom in AI-related science publications, the report also lists a host of newly released science foundation models — AI models that are broadly trained to take on a wide range of tasks, but also specially trained on massive data sets from a specific domain of science.

Many researchers have started to rely on AI ‘agents’ that autonomously carry out actions including scientific workflows, but the report is sceptical about their performance. AI agents still struggle to reliably perform multistep workflows, it reports, with the best AI agents scoring roughly half as well as human specialists with PhDs. “Agents are wonderful, but we are still far from a place where we understand how to use them effectively,” says Gil.

Growing numbers

The report says that in 2025, more than 80,000 papers, preprints and other types of publication in the natural sciences — which includes life, physical and Earth sciences — mentioned AI, 26% more than in 2024. The subcategory of physical sciences had the largest number of publications that mention AI (33,000). The Earth sciences category had the highest percentage (9%).

How AI agents will change research: a scientist’s guide

The boom in AI-related science papers is “not surprising”, says computer scientist Arvind Narayanan at Princeton University in New Jersey, who was not involved with creating the index. But it’s not clear, he says, whether the rise of AI use is productive for science. “Whether or not this explosive growth is meaningful is hotly debated,” Narayanan says. “My view is that it is happening too fast, without giving scientific norms time to adjust, and so the quality of research has taken a nosedive.”

Gil says there isn’t much evidence yet that AI is improving scientists’ productivity. “The studies are limited,” she says. But, she adds, scientists “can’t live without it. If you took AI away from them, there would be a riot. So it must be helping in some way.”

Top models

As uptake has grown, so has the number of AI platforms available to researchers. This past year saw the emergence of many science foundation models, including the first one for astronomy, AION-1, which was trained on more than 200 million celestial objects. This training helps it to classify galaxies or estimate their properties. “When I talked to scientists in 2024 and said ‘There’s foundation models for science’, scientists would not know what that means. They didn’t know they existed. I think we have seen that really advance very quickly,” says Gil.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and measurable increase in the adoption of AI in natural sciences, highlighting broad scientific engagement with AI technologies and the emergence of specialized AI foundation models that can advance research. Despite current limitations in AI agent performance, the rapid growth and integration of AI tools in scientific research represent a meaningful technological breakthrough with potential to broadly benefit society through accelerated scientific discovery.
articles.personality_title: Scientists rapidly adopt AI, though humans still lead on complex tasks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Over the past 15 years, artificial intelligence (AI) has become more common in natural science research fields like biology, physics, and Earth science. Researchers are using AI tools to help with their work, but how well these tools perform compared to human experts is still being studied.

**What happened** – A new report from Stanford University shows that the number of scientific papers mentioning AI grew nearly 30 times from 2010 to 2025. In 2025 alone, over 80,000 natural science papers included AI. Scientists have also created special AI models trained on large scientific data, such as one for astronomy called AION-1, which helps classify galaxies.

**Impact** – This growth shows scientists are eager to use AI, but the report finds that AI agents—programs that perform scientific tasks on their own—are still not as good as human experts. These AI agents complete complex, multi-step tasks only about half as well as specialists with PhDs. Still, many scientists rely on AI daily and find it useful.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving AI models and agents to make them more reliable and effective. As AI tools develop, scientists hope they will speed up discoveries in many fields. At the same time, experts are watching carefully to ensure AI helps maintain high research quality.

**One-sentence takeaway** – While AI is becoming a key tool in science, human experts remain better at complex tasks, and ongoing improvements aim to make AI more helpful in research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques adoptent rapidement l’IA, mais les humains restent meilleurs pour les tâches complexes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 15 ans, l’intelligence artificielle (IA) est de plus en plus utilisée dans les domaines des sciences naturelles comme la biologie, la physique et les sciences de la Terre. Les chercheurs utilisent des outils d’IA pour les aider, mais leur performance comparée aux experts humains est encore étudiée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau rapport de l’Université de Stanford montre que le nombre d’articles scientifiques mentionnant l’IA a été multiplié par près de 30 entre 2010 et 2025. En 2025, plus de 80 000 articles en sciences naturelles incluaient l’IA. Des modèles d’IA spécialisés ont aussi été créés, comme AION-1 en astronomie, qui aide à classer les galaxies.

**Impact** – Cette croissance montre que les scientifiques adoptent l’IA avec enthousiasme, mais le rapport révèle que les agents d’IA – des programmes qui réalisent des tâches scientifiques de manière autonome – sont encore moins performants que les experts humains. Ces agents réussissent des tâches complexes environ deux fois moins bien que des spécialistes titulaires d’un doctorat. Pourtant, beaucoup de scientifiques utilisent l’IA quotidiennement et la trouvent utile.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les modèles et agents d’IA pour les rendre plus fiables et efficaces. Avec ces progrès, l’IA pourrait accélérer les découvertes dans plusieurs domaines. En même temps, les experts surveillent la qualité des recherches pour éviter une baisse liée à l’usage rapide de l’IA.

**Phrase clé** – Bien que l’IA devienne un outil important en science, les experts humains restent meilleurs pour les tâches complexes, et les progrès visent à rendre l’IA plus utile dans la recherche.
articles.personality_title_es: Los científicos adoptan rápidamente la IA, pero los humanos siguen mejor en tareas complejas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los últimos 15 años, la inteligencia artificial (IA) se ha vuelto más común en la investigación de ciencias naturales como biología, física y ciencias de la Tierra. Los investigadores usan herramientas de IA para ayudar en su trabajo, pero aún se estudia qué tan bien funcionan comparadas con expertos humanos.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo informe de la Universidad de Stanford muestra que el número de artículos científicos que mencionan la IA creció casi 30 veces entre 2010 y 2025. Solo en 2025, más de 80,000 artículos de ciencias naturales incluyeron IA. También se crearon modelos de IA especializados, como AION-1 en astronomía, que ayuda a clasificar galaxias.

**Impacto** – Este crecimiento muestra que los científicos están usando la IA con entusiasmo, pero el informe señala que los agentes de IA — programas que realizan tareas científicas de forma autónoma — aún no son tan buenos como los expertos humanos. Estos agentes completan tareas complejas aproximadamente a la mitad del nivel de especialistas con doctorado. Sin embargo, muchos científicos usan la IA diariamente y la encuentran útil.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando los modelos y agentes de IA para hacerlos más fiables y efectivos. A medida que estas herramientas se desarrollen, se espera que aceleren los descubrimientos en varias áreas. Al mismo tiempo, los expertos vigilan que la calidad de la investigación se mantenga alta.

**Frase clave** – Aunque la IA se está convirtiendo en una herramienta clave en la ciencia, los expertos humanos siguen siendo mejores en tareas complejas, y se trabaja para que la IA sea más útil en la investigación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, open book filled with swirling scientific symbols and starry galaxies rising from its pages, surrounded by softly illuminated, interconnected neural network patterns that gently intertwine with delicate, abstract representations of AI circuits and human silhouettes made of light, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.
